---
title: "[SOLVED] /dev/scd0 or /dev/scd1??"
date: 2008-12-22
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by santiagorf on 2008-12-22
I have two DVD drives (calle them A and B), and every time I reboot my computer my two DVD drives may change their names. 
Thus, when I want use unit "A", I don't know beforehand if it is either */dev/scd**0***, or  */dev/scd**1***.
Are there any way to avoid this problem?

Thanks in advance
santiagorf



PS: I don't know if it helps bu here is my fstab

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=f43c529c-8fa8-4f1a-aa15-0fac821958a1 / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro,relatime 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=ECF0E363F0E3328E /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
UUID=f13a09c1-a2b1-4165-afba-382183d0b155 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/hda0 /media/hda0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec 0 0
/dev/hda1 /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda2 /media/sda2 ext3 defaults,relatime 0 0
/dev/sda5 /media/sda5 swap defaults 0 0

Thus, when I want use unit "A", I don't know beforehand if it is either */dev/scd**0***, or  */dev/scd**1***.
Are there any way to avoid this problem?

Thanks in advance
santiagorf



PS: I don't know if it helps bu here is my fstab

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=f43c529c-8fa8-4f1a-aa15-0fac821958a1 / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro,relatime 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=ECF0E363F0E3328E /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
**[COLOR="Blue"]UUID=f13a09c1-a2b1-4165-afba-382183d0b155 none swap sw 0 0[/COLOR]**
/dev/hda0 /media/hda0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec 0 0
/dev/hda1 /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda2 /media/sda2 ext3 defaults,relatime 0 0
**[COLOR="Blue"]/dev/sda5 /media/sda5 swap defaults 0 0[/COLOR]**

You don't have any entry for your CD/DVD in /etc/fstab?

Also, the last entry looks just wrong.  Is /dev/sda5 your swap partition?

```
sudo fdisk -l
sudo blkid
free 
df -h
```

---

### Post by taurus on 2008-12-22
> **santiagorf said:**
> 
df -h
Filesystem            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
**[COLOR="Blue"]/dev/sda2              46G  8.6G   35G  20% /[/COLOR]**
tmpfs                 253M     0  253M   0% /lib/init/rw
varrun                253M  196K  253M   1% /var/run
varlock               253M     0  253M   0% /var/lock
udev                  253M  640K  252M   1% /dev
tmpfs                 253M     0  253M   0% /dev/shm
[B][COLOR="Red"]/dev/sda1              29G   20G  9.4G  68% /media/hda1
/dev/sda1              29G   20G  9.4G  68% /media/sda1[/COLOR][/B]
**[COLOR="Blue"]/dev/sda2              46G  8.6G   35G  20% /media/sda2[/COLOR]**

You have a couple of duplicate partitions.

```

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config --
#
# <file system> <mount point> <type> <options> <dump> <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda2 :
UUID=f43c529c-8fa8-4f1a-aa15-0fac821958a1 / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro,relatime 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda1 :
UUID=ECF0E363F0E3328E /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
UUID=f13a09c1-a2b1-4165-afba-382183d0b155 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/hda0 /media/hda0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec 0 0
/dev/hda1 /media/hda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
[B][COLOR="Purple"]/dev/sda1 /media/sda1 ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_CA.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda2 /media/sda2 ext3 defaults,relatime 0 0[/COLOR][/B]

```
So, you need to edit /etc/fstab

```
gksudo gedit /etc/fstab
```
and remove the last two lines.  Then, add these two lines at the end for your two CD/DVD drives.

```

/dev/scd0       /media/cdrom0   udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0       0
/dev/scd1       /media/cdrom1   udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0       0

```
Save it.  Then, see if you have /media/cdrom0 & /media/cdrom1.

```
ls -la /media
```
If you don't, then create those two new mount points.

```
sudo mkdir /media/cdrom0 /media/cdrom1
```
Reboot.

---

### Post by santiagorf on 2008-12-22
it seems it works well
thank you!!

---
